content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive/Legal • • • Enforcement • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-4441          322-6444

May 15, 1981

Stephen T. Huhn
International Business Machines Corp.
3424 Wilshire Blvd.
Los Angeles, CA 90010

Dear Mr. Huhn:

This is in response to your letter of May 4, 1981 requesting confirmation of the facts stated in your letter and discussed
in our meeting of April 29, 1981.

Your question was whether an employee of IBM who is a registered lobbyist and also a marketing representative must report
as compensation for lobbying any portion of this compensation received by commissions contingent upon normal sales activity.

The lobbyist would not be required to report on his lobbyist
statement any compensation for activities not in connection with
his activities as a lobbyist (i.e. compensation for activities
as a marketing representative that is contingent upon normal sales
activity.

However, Government Code Section 86205 (f) states that no
lobbyist shall "accept or agree to accept any payment in any way
contingent upon the defeat, enactment or outcome of any proposed
legislative or administrative action." There may be instances
where the prohibition contained in Section 86205 (f) may prevent
a person from serving both as a marketing representative and a
lobbyist with regard to a particular transaction. Because of the
hypothetical nature of the question, I cannot provide a definitive
answer. If a situation arises that may come within the prohibition,
please contact the Commission for advice at that time.

Yours Very Truly,

Lynn Montgomery
Political Reform
Consultant

IBM International Business Machines Corporation

Office of Legal Counsel                                May 7 11 31 AM '81
Data Processing Division                               3424 Wilshire Blvd., Los Angeles, Cal. 90010


May 4, 1981


Ms. Lynn Montgomery
Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 Kay Street
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Ms. Montgomery:

Thank you very much for the opportunity to meet with you on April 29.
In order to confirm our discussion of that date I would like to again
relate herein the facts of our situation and seek confirmation from you
that our interpretation of the law is correct.

Our company has designated one of our marketing representatives to be
our registered lobbyist in the State of California. This individual is
assigned duties from time to time which fall within the statutory de-
finition of lobbying. He has registered with the state and files regular
reports of lobbying activity, as required.

By far the greatest amount of our registered lobbyist's time is devoted
to non-lobbying activities, that is, normal marketing activity in pro-
moting the use of our products at various administrative agencies.  This
activity may include identifying agency problems and potential solutions
which our equipment may offer, presenting proposals and product announcements
to agencies, presenting cost benefit analyses, and responding to solicitation
documents which may be let by various agencies.

Our registered lobbyist has engaged in the following types of lobbying
activities: conversations with members of the legislature, their staffs
and various administrative agencies on the subject of procurement pro-
cedures, as they may be legislatively established, budget matters, and
discussion of changes in procedures specified by the State Administrative
Manual, or the Administrative Code or Register.

Currently our representative is compensated almost entirely through
a stated annual salary. In order to bring this individual's com-
pensation plan into parity with that of other employees of our company,
we desire to compensate him in two ways: (1) through an annual base
salary; (2) by commission contingent upon his normal sales activity.

Ms. Lynn Montgomery
Page 2
May 4, 1981


Commissions would be contingent only upon the success of his marketing
responsibilities, and would in no way be contingent upon success of
lobbying responsibilities. For instance, entitlement to commissions would
occur either upon confirmed sale of equipment, and/or installation of
such equipment. The event which would trigger payment of commissions
could only occur following an administrative decision of an agency to
make a contract award. Our representative's statement of responsibilities
would clearly state that compensation for lobbying activities would come
only from the allocated portion of his base salary.

I would appreciate your written confirmation that my interpretation of
the law is correct, i.e., the law does not prohibit payment of commissions
to a registered lobbyist when payment is contingent upon his non-registered
activities.

Again, thank you very much for the opportunity to meet with you and
for your help in this matter.

Very truly yours,

Stephen T. Huhn
Stephen T. Huhn

However, Government Code Section 86205 (f) states that no lobbyist shall "accept or agree to accept any payment in any way contingent upon the defeat, enactment or outcome of any proposed legislative or administrative action." There may be instances where the prohibition contained in Section 86205 (f) may prevent a person from serving both as a marketing representative and a lobbyist with regard to a particular transaction. Because of the hypothetical nature of the question, I cannot provide a definitive answer.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a registered lobbyist who also serves as a marketing representative must report commission compensation contingent on normal sales activity as lobbying compensation.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Non-lobbying compensation contingent on normal sales activity need not be reported as lobbying compensation, but Government Code Section 86205(f) prohibits payments contingent on legislative/administrative outcomes. The FPPC cannot provide a definitive answer without specific facts about whether a particular transaction triggers this prohibition.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: old; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (May 4, 1981) from Stephen T. Huhn at IBM and the FPPC response letter (May 15, 1981) from Lynn Montgomery. Response is conditional/advisory in nature due to hypothetical aspects of the question. Minor OCR issues present but do not significantly impact extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Your question was whether an employee of IBM who is a registered lobbyist and also a marketing representative must report as compensation for lobbying any portion of this compensation received by commissions contingent upon normal sales activity.

CONCLUSION: The lobbyist would not be required to report on his lobbyist statement any compensation for activities not in connection with his activities as a lobbyist (i.e. compensation for activities as a marketing representative that is contingent upon normal sales activity. However, Government Code Section 86205 (f) states that no lobbyist shall "accept or agree to accept any payment in any way contingent upon the defeat, enactment or outcome of any proposed legislative or administrative action." There may be instances where the prohibition contained in Section 86205 (f) may prevent a person from serving both as a marketing representative and a lobbyist with regard to a particular transaction. Because of the hypothetical nature of the question, I cannot provide a definitive answer.

embedding.summary: IBM requested confirmation that commission compensation to a registered lobbyist contingent only on marketing sales activity (not lobbying success) need not be reported as lobbying compensation. FPPC responded that such non-lobbying compensation need not be reported, but cautioned that Government Code Section 86205(f) may prevent dual service in certain transactions and advised contact for specific situations.
